title: Networking Essentials
subtitle: OSI model, WebSockets, load balancers, and how data moves across networks

sections.title: The OSI Model
sections.text:
The OSI model describes how data travels across a network in 7 layers. You only need to care about a few:

  • Layer 7 (Application) — Where your code lives. HTTP, DNS, SMTP.
  • Layer 4 (Transport) — TCP (guaranteed delivery) and UDP (fast, no guarantee). Ports live here.
  • Layer 3 (Network) — IP routing. Getting data from your computer to a server across the world.
  • Layers 1-2 — Physical cables and Wi-Fi. Rarely your problem.

The internet actually uses TCP/IP (a simpler 4-layer model), but OSI helps you debug: "Can't reach the website? Is it Layer 7 (server crashed?), Layer 4 (port blocked?), or Layer 3 (IP unreachable?)
sections.caption: The 7-layer OSI model — data moves from application to physical and back
sections.detail: The OSI model is a teaching tool, not a strict implementation guide. The Internet uses TCP/IP (a 4-layer model: Link → Internet → Transport → Application). But understanding OSI helps diagnose network issues. If you can't reach a website: layer 7 (is the server running?), layer 4 (is the port open?), layer 3 (is the IP reachable?), layer 1 (is the cable plugged in?).
sections.code:
# Diagnose network issues by OSI layer

# Layer 3 - is the host reachable?
$ ping google.com

# Layer 4 - is the port open?
$ nc -zv google.com 80

# Layer 7 - is the web server responding?
$ curl -I https://google.com

sections.title: HTTP vs WebSockets
sections.text:
HTTP is like sending a letter — you write, they respond, conversation over. The client asks, the server answers. Great for APIs and web pages where the client controls when data is requested.

WebSockets is like a phone call — the line stays open. Both sides can talk at any time. The server can push messages to the client without waiting for a request. This is what makes live chat, real-time notifications, collaborative editing, and gaming possible.

The WebSocket connection starts as HTTP (an Upgrade: websocket header), then switches protocols. This lets it work through standard firewalls and proxies.

items: Best for: REST APIs, web pages
Server can push data to client
Best for: chat, gaming, live data

sections.caption: HTTP for request-response, WebSockets for real-time bidirectional communication

sections.title: Load Balancers
sections.text:
A load balancer sits in front of your servers and distributes traffic. It's like having a host at a restaurant who decides which waiter takes your order. If one server goes down, the load balancer stops sending traffic to it — your users never notice.

Common strategies:

  • Round Robin — Take turns. Simple, but ignores actual server load.
  • Least Connections — Send to the least busy server. Smarter.
  • IP Hash — Same user always goes to the same server (for sticky sessions).

Load balancers also handle SSL termination (decrypting HTTPS before it reaches your servers) and health checks (pinging servers to make sure they're alive).
sections.caption: Load balancer distributes traffic across healthy servers, routing around failures
sections.callout: Load balancers are the first line of defense for scaling. They handle SSL termination, health checks, and can prevent the thundering herd problem during server restarts.
